Unique Baby Name Generator

Discover rare and uncommon baby names. Filter by gender and set a maximum popularity threshold to surface hidden-gem names from 100,000+ real records.

Want a fully random name instead? Try the Random Name Generator. Looking for meaning? Name Meaning Lookup covers etymology and origin.

Try a preset

What is a Unique Baby Name Generator?

A unique baby name generator filters a real-name database by rarity and gender to surface names that are uncommon but still authentic. The HowManyOfMe generator pulls from 100,000+ birth-registry names from sources like the Social Security Administration, lets you set a hard popularity ceiling (under 1,000, 5,000, 10,000 or 50,000 bearers), and returns 12 fresh suggestions per click — no AI hallucinations, only real recorded names. Want to see how many people have your name before looking for unique ones?

Key Features and Attributes

Real-name database

100,000+ verified names from US, UK and 78 other national registries — never AI-invented.

Adjustable rarity slider

Four thresholds from 'very rare' (<1k bearers) to 'distinctive' (<50k).

Gender filter

Boy, Girl, or Any (which includes unisex).

12 fresh suggestions per click

Results re-shuffle every generation so you never see the same set twice.

One-click presets

Curated combos like 'Hidden gems' and 'Rare boy names' for instant results.

Direct stats links

Tap any suggestion to view its full popularity, meaning and origin page.

Who Uses This Tool?

Parents avoiding top-10 names

Scenario: Don't want their child to be 'Emma R.' in a class of 4 Emmas.

Result: Set max popularity to 5,000 and generate truly distinctive options.

Authors naming side characters

Scenario: Need 12 background characters with believable but non-generic names.

Result: One click yields 12 real but rare names — perfect for ensemble casts.

Game/D&D character creators

Scenario: Want fantasy-feeling names that aren't fantasy clichés.

Result: Real rare names like 'Atlas', 'Wren', 'Cleo' work better than invented ones.

Brand/pet namers

Scenario: Looking for a memorable brand or pet name with no Google competition.

Result: Names below 1,000 bearers globally have near-zero SEO competition.

Worked Examples

Input: "Boy + under 1k"

Finding: Returned: Knox, Cassius, Wilder, Bowen, Forrest…

Insight: Each has fewer than 1,000 known bearers — genuinely rare yet pronounceable.

Input: "Girl + under 5k"

Finding: Returned: Wren, Cleo, Iris, Marlowe, Saoirse…

Insight: Vintage and nature-inspired names dominate this rarity tier.

Input: "Any + under 50k"

Finding: Returned: Atlas, Sage, River, Quinn, Phoenix…

Insight: Unisex modern names — distinctive but not yet saturated.

✓ Pros

  • ✓ 100% real recorded names, no AI hallucinations
  • ✓ Hard rarity filter you control
  • ✓ One-click presets save time
  • ✓ Free, unlimited, no signup
  • ✓ Direct links to full name statistics

✗ Cons

  • ✗ Names below 1,000 bearers may be very hard to spell or pronounce
  • ✗ Today's rare name may become tomorrow's trendy name (Aria, Atlas effect)
  • ✗ Results favour Western naming traditions; non-Western coverage is growing

Frequently Asked Questions

What counts as a 'unique' baby name?▾

We define unique as a name given to fewer than 1 in 10,000 babies in a given year — roughly any name outside the top 1,000. You can tighten this with the Max Popularity slider.

Are unique names better than common names?▾

Neither is objectively better. Unique names give individuality but require more spelling explanations; common names blend in but mean shared classroom names. Choose based on what you value.

How do you find rare baby names?▾

Filter our 100,000-name database by gender and a maximum bearer count. Anything below 1,000 bearers is genuinely rare; below 100 is essentially unique.

Why are unique names becoming popular?▾

Social media and globalization have driven a 30-year shift away from top-10 names. In the 1950s, the top 10 boy names covered 35% of all births; today they cover under 10%.

Can a rare name become popular later?▾

Yes — names like 'Aria', 'Atlas' and 'Luna' were rare a decade ago and are now in the top 100. The unique-today/popular-tomorrow risk is real.

Will my child have trouble with a rare name?▾

Studies show pronunciation/spelling friction is the main downside, but research also links uncommon names to higher creativity scores. Trade-offs exist either way.

Is the Unique Name Generator free?▾

Yes — unlimited generations, no signup, no email, no paywall.

Can I generate unisex unique names?▾

Yes. Set Gender to 'Any' and the generator includes all unisex names in its candidate pool.

Where do these unique names come from?▾

All suggestions are real recorded names from US, UK and 78 other national birth registries — never AI-invented strings.

How is this different from a baby name book?▾

Books are static lists. Our generator uses live popularity filters so you only see names that are actually rare today, not names that were rare in 1995.
